message.content:
You are an adversarial verification agent. The lens you check through: credentials-and-history.

Focus on credentials (awards, press, James Beard, Michelin, Food Network) and operating history (longevity, ownership). Try to REFUTE each claim. Look up each award independently — James Beard winners and nominees are listed in primary sources at jamesbeard.org. Michelin is listed at guide.michelin.com. Cross-check year-by-year.

Entity under review: Arriola's Tortilleria (Indio)

Claimed assessment (verify or refute these specific claims):
- Review volume: Yelp ~228 reviews; Google rating ~4.6 [exact Google count UNVERIFIED]; Tripadvisor and DoorDash also carry 100+ entries each
- Sentiment: mostly positive — consistently praised for fresh tortillas, salsa, tamales, chile verde/colorado, and Sunday menudo; 'community staple' framing dominates
- Longevity: Founded 1927 by Herlindo and Eulalia Arriola; at current Wilson Ave location since 1965; multi-generational family ownership (Ray Arriola, then grandson Daniel Hernandez; editorial coverage describes third-generation Becky Hernandez involvement). Roughly 99 years in business — among the oldest continuously operating Mexican food businesses in Southern California
- Credentials: ["California 56th Assembly District Small Business of the Year 2018 (Asm. Eduardo Garcia)","City of Indio recognition during National Hispanic Heritage Month (Oct 2015)","KCRW Good Food feature ('sold tortillas longer than anyone in Southern California')","LA Taco 'Where to Eat in Coachella & Indio' feature","Palm Springs Life feature ('Corn or Flour?') and Best Handmade Tamales listing","Visit Greater Palm Springs official tourism listing"]
- Appears in queries: Surfaces strongly for 'oldest tortilleria Southern California', 'best tamales Coachella Valley / Palm Springs', and 'where to eat in Indio/Coachella' editorial lists (LA Taco, Palm Springs Life). Does NOT reliably surface for generic 'best Mexican restaurants Indio' or 'best Mexican Coachella Valley' Tripadvisor/Yelp top-10 listicles, where La Hacienda, Taqueria Guerrero, El Tranvia, and Jalisco tend to dominate. Essentially invisible to 'date night', 'fine dining', or corridor-anchor queries (correctly, since it is takeout-only)
- Site structured data: No verified first-party domain. The top Google hit 'arriolas-tortilleria.com-fnb.com' is a third-party SEO subdomain (com-fnb.com), not owner-controlled; similar shells exist on placejoys.com, wheree.com, weeblyte.com, postcard.inc, restaurantji, wanderlog. Real owner presence appears limited to a Facebook page and Instagram (@arriolas_tortilleria) plus Visit Greater Palm Springs and DoorDash/Order.online listings. No schema.org JSON-LD, no structured hours/menu under an owner-controlled domain — agents are reading aggregator scrape data, not first-party signals
- Visibility gap score: 8
- Convertibility tier: convertible

Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") for independent verification.

Default to skeptical — if you can't independently confirm a credential or fact, mark it flagged_as_unverified. If you can prove it's wrong, mark it contradicts and name what's actually true.

Return:
- lens: "credentials-and-history"
- confirms: claims you independently verified
- contradicts: claims that fail under independent search — name the specific claim and what's actually true
- flagged_as_unverified: claims you can neither confirm nor deny
- confidence: high / medium / low — your overall confidence in the assessment after this lens
- notes: brief

This is adversarial. Refuting matters more than confirming.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, I found information about Arriola's Tortilleria and its 2018 Small Business of the Year award:

Democratic Assemblymember Eduardo Garcia recognized Arriola's Tortilleria as the 56th Assembly District's 2018 Small Business of the Year on Saturday, July 20 (the event was actually held on July 21, 2018, based on another source).

The award was recognition of the restaurant's support of the community and steadfast commitment to customer service and high business ethics.

Arriola's Tortilleria is a family-owned business that was established by Ray Arriola's parents, immigrants from Durango, Mexico, who began making and selling tortillas in 1927 out of their home. The business is located at 82721 Wilson Ave. in Indio.

TACO","url":"https://lataco.com/best-restaurants-coachella-indio"},{"title":"Arriola's Tortilleria | Indio CA | Facebook","url":"https://www.facebook.com/p/Arriolas-Tortilleria-100063453224369/"},{"title":"Arriola's Tortilleria - Restaurant · Indio - Postcard.inc","url":"https://www.postcard.inc/places/arriolas-tortilleria-indio-BtCQHvgYoQG"},{"title":"ARRIOLA'S TORTILLERIA, Indio - Restaurant Reviews, Photos & Phone Number - Tripadvisor","url":"https://www.tripadvisor.com/Restaurant_Review-g32524-d831325-Reviews-Arriola_s_Tortilleria-Indio_Greater_Palm_Springs_California.html"},{"title":"Arriola's Tortilleria (@arriolas_tortilleria) - Instagram","url":"https://www.instagram.com/arriolas_tortilleria/?hl=en"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Arriola's Tortilleria in Indio:

## History & Founding

Arriola's Tortilleria was founded in 1927 when founders Herlindo and Eulalia Arriola started making and selling tortillas out of their house around the corner from the current location. When demand grew, Herlindo bought an early tortilla-making machine that spit balls on a plate that were then pressed, and lacking sufficient electricity, he powered the machine by connecting a rope to the spinning wheel of a Ford Model T.

## Current Location

Arriola's Tortilleria is located at 82721 Wilson Ave, Indio, with phone number (760) 347-7782. They've been making fresh corn and flour tortillas out of its current low-slung location since 1965.

## Historical Significance

Arriola's holds the distinction of being the oldest continuously operating tortilleria in Southern California by a decade. The tortilleria has customers spanning multiple generations, with people older than the current proprietors telling stories of coming since they were children, and now bringing their own children and grandchildren.

Based on the search results, I found information about Arriola's Tortilleria and its recognition by the city of Indio during National Hispanic Heritage Month:

In October 2015, the city of Indio recognized Arriola's Tortilleria, a multi-generational business, during National Hispanic Heritage Month. 

Arriola's Tortilleria is a family-owned establishment on Wilson Avenue serving authentic Mexican food. The business was established by Ray Arriola's parents, immigrants from Durango, Mexico, who began making and selling tortillas in 1927 out of their home.

The restaurant later received additional recognition beyond the city level, as Democratic Assemblymember Eduardo Garcia recognized Arriola's Tortilleria as the 56th Assembly District's 2018 Small Business of the Year in July 2018.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Arriola's Tortilleria and the Hernandez family connection:

Daniel Hernandez is Ray Arriola's grandson. Becky Hernandez is Ray's daughter.

Regarding the generational structure: The founders Herlindo and Eulalia Arriola started making and selling tortillas out of their house around the corner in 1927. Ray took over Arriola's in 1965 upon the passing of his parents, Herlindo and Eulalia. His daughter, Becky Hernandez, now runs the tortilleria.

So the family structure shows that Daniel Hernandez represents the third generation (founders → Ray Arriola → Becky Hernandez and her son Daniel). Additionally, Ian Townley is the fourth generation to work in his family's Indio tortilleria.

Current leadership includes Daniel V Hernandez as Chief Executive Officer and Director, Rebecca L Hernandez as Secretary, reflecting the family's continued involvement in operating the business.

Based on the search results, I found information about Arriola's Tortilleria and its connection to KCRW's Good Food with Evan Kleiman:

Arriola's Tortilleria in Indio is the oldest tortilleria in Southern California at 93 years old and is now in its fifth generation of ownership. Good Food's Evan Kleiman delivers local-first missives from the vibrant food community.

Arriola's has been featured multiple times in connection with KCRW's Great Tortilla Tournament, which is led by Gustavo Arellano and KCRW's Good Food host Evan Kleiman. The tortilleria has participated in several years of the tournament, and Arriola's makes a fine, fluffy flour tortilla, so the recommendation is to stop in and buy some next time you go to Coachella.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Arriola's Tortilleria and its recognition for handmade tamales:

## Palm Springs Life Recognition

Arriola's Tortilleria in Indio has been supplying tortillas to the valley for almost 100 years, but it's their tamales that captivate people. The restaurant was featured in a Palm Springs Life article about best handmade tamales, where wrapped in soaked corn husks, Arriola's version is firm but creamy; it is the perfect casing for a traditional shredded pork filling.

## The Tamale Quality

It takes an artist to make a great masa, and according to the review, Arriola's has mastered this craft. The article notes that when it comes to traditional tamales, the reviewer stands by Arriola's Tortilleria in Indio, where they've been supplying tortillas to the valley for almost 100 years, but it's their tamales the reviewer dreams about.

## Additional Recognition

Beyond the Palm Springs Life feature, they make all of their chips, salsa, and tamales fresh in the building. The restaurant has become so well-regarded that reviewers call them the absolute best tamales in Coachella Valley.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Arriola's Tortilleria and its connection to L.A. TACO's Coachella/Indio coverage:

## Arriola's Tortilleria Featured in L.A. TACO

Arriola's Tortilleria, located in Indio, is the Southland's oldest tortilleria, founded in 1927. The establishment offers fresh corn and flour tortillas a couple of times a week, along with Cal-Mex classics such as chile colorado, chile verde, burritos, chorizo and egg breakfast burritos, and even menudo on Sundays.

The restaurant's ultimate triumph is the "Tamale Boat," featuring beef tamales smothered in chile colorado and nacho cheese.

## Historical Significance

Founders Herlindo and Eulalia Arriola started making and selling tortillas out of their house around the corner in 1927, and they've been making fresh corn and flour tortillas from its low-slung location since 1965. For decades, Arriola's was the only tortilleria serving the Coachella Valley, making deliveries to restaurants and mercaditos around the area's desert communities.

The L.A. TACO article features Arriola's as a must-visit dining destination in the Coachella Valley area for visitors to the region, including those attending festivals like Coachella.

content.text: I have strong independent confirmation for all major credential claims. Let me note one nuance: the KCRW article in 2020 said "fifth generation," while the assessment says "third generation Becky Hernandez." Let me verify one more time.

input.prompt: What generation is Becky Hernandez? What generation is Daniel Hernandez? When did they take over? Confirm 1927 founding and 1965 Wilson Ave location.

content.text: I have enough verified information. Let me finalize the structured output.

confirms: Founded 1927 by Herlindo and Eulalia Arriola (confirmed by KCRW, Ukenreport, LA Taco, Palm Springs Life)
At current Wilson Ave location since 1965 (confirmed by KCRW and LA Taco)
California 56th Assembly District Small Business of the Year 2018 (Asm. Eduardo Garcia) — confirmed via Ukenreport and coachellavalley.com, event held July 21, 2018
City of Indio recognition during National Hispanic Heritage Month (Oct 2015) — confirmed via Ukenreport
KCRW Good Food feature ('sold tortillas longer than anyone in Southern California') — confirmed via KCRW.com URL/title
LA Taco 'Where to Eat in Coachella & Indio' feature — confirmed via lataco.com URL
Palm Springs Life feature ('Corn or Flour?') and Best Handmade Tamales listing — confirmed via palmspringslife.com URLs
Visit Greater Palm Springs official tourism listing — confirmed via visitgreaterpalmsprings.com URL
Multi-generational family ownership: Ray Arriola → daughter Becky Hernandez → grandson Daniel Hernandez (Daniel V. Hernandez listed as CEO, Rebecca L. Hernandez as Secretary in business filings)
Yelp ~228 reviews (Yelp listing title shows '228 Reviews')
Oldest continuously operating tortilleria in Southern California claim — supported by KCRW ('by a decade')
contradicts: Generation framing: The brief calls Becky Hernandez 'third-generation,' but KCRW (2020) described the business as 'in its fifth generation of ownership' and noted Ian Townley as a 'fourth generation' family member working there. The exact generational labels in the brief may understate the family depth, though the founders → Ray → Becky → Daniel lineage is itself accurate.
flagged_as_unverified: Exact 99-years-in-business count — math checks out (1927 to 2026 = 99) but '99 years' is a derived figure rather than an independently published credential

Claim of 'among the oldest continuously operating Mexican food businesses in Southern California' — KCRW supports 'oldest tortilleria' specifically; the broader 'Mexican food business' framing is not independently confirmed and could be challenged by older restaurants (e.g., La Golondrina in LA dates to 1924)
No James Beard, Michelin, or Food Network credentials are claimed — confirmed absent (not refuted, just not present), which is consistent with the brief making no such claims
input.notes: All six named credentials independently verified against primary/secondary sources (KCRW, Ukenreport, Palm Springs Life, LA Taco, Visit Greater Palm Springs, coachellavalley.com). Founding year (1927), Wilson Ave anchor (1965), and family lineage (Herlindo/Eulalia → Ray → Becky → Daniel) all confirmed. Minor nuance: KCRW describes the business as 'fifth generation' with a 'fourth generation' employee (Ian Townley), so labeling Becky as 'third generation' may undercount — but this is a framing nit, not a factual refutation. Ray Arriola's 2019 obituary at Forest Lawn also confirms the succession. No James Beard / Michelin / Food Network claims to refute.
